title: Climate Leadership for Community Action Virtual Course 2026: Free Online Climate Leadership Training with Certificate for Youth in Africa and the Middle East
summary: Climate change is one of the defining challenges of our generation, and young people are increasingly taking the lead in creating innovative, community-driven solutions. To support the next generation of climate leaders, DOT has launched applications for the Climate Leadership for Community Action Virtual Course 2026 , a fully online learning experience designed to equip youth with practical climate leadership skills, advocacy techniques, and community engagement strategies. This six-week virtual course offers participants the opportunity to learn alongside peers from eight countries across Africa and the Middle East while receiving mentorship from experienced Climate Ambassadors. Whether you are passionate about environmental sustainability, climate justice, or community development, this course provides the knowledge and practical tools needed to design and implement impactful climate action projects. Unlike many online courses that focus only on theory, this program emphasizes practical application through a proven Participatory Action Research (PAR) approach, helping participants translate climate knowledge into real-world community action. If you are ready to become a climate leader, this is an excellent opportunity to develop valuable skills, earn a recognized certificate, and join an international network of young climate advocates. About the Climate Leadership for Community Action Virtual Course The Climate Leadership for Community Action Virtual Course is a cohort-based online training program developed by DOT to empower young people to become effective climate leaders within their communities. Participants will not simply complete an online course individually. Instead, they will become members of a collaborative international learning community where they will: Learn from experienced climate practitioners Work alongside youth from multiple countries Participate in interactive discussions Receive coaching from Climate Ambassadors Develop practical community climate solutions The program is designed around the belief that lasting climate action begins at the community level and that young people are uniquely positioned to drive sustainable change. Importantly, no previous climate experience is required . Anyone with the passion to learn and contribute to climate action is encouraged to apply. Why This Climate Leadership Course Matters Communities around the world are increasingly facing the impacts of climate change, including: Rising temperatures Extreme weather events Food insecurity Water scarcity Environmental degradation Economic disruption Young people are among those most affected by these challenges, yet they are also becoming some of the strongest advocates for climate solutions. This virtual course equips participants with practical leadership skills so they can: Understand climate science Engage local communities Advocate for climate policies Design sustainable projects Promote climate justice Build resilience within vulnerable communities Rather than focusing solely on environmental theory, the course helps participants become active problem-solvers. Program Duration The Climate Leadership for Community Action Virtual Course runs for: Duration: 6 weeks Weekly commitment: Approximately 5 hours Total learning time: Around 30 hours The structure is designed to accommodate students, professionals, and young leaders with existing academic or work commitments. Course Format The program is delivered entirely online and follows a cohort-based learning model. Participants will benefit from: Interactive online learning Live coaching sessions Facilitated discussions Collaborative learning Peer networking Guided practical assignments Throughout the course, learners receive ongoing support from DOT Climate Ambassadors who provide mentorship and guidance. Course Start Date The next cohort begins on: Start Date: 15 July 2026 Participants will receive onboarding information before the course officially begins. Certificate of Completion Participants who successfully complete the course requirements will receive an official certificate. Certificate Award Date: By 9 September 2026 The certificate recognizes participants successful completion of the Climate Leadership for Community Action program. What Participants Will Learn The curriculum combines climate knowledge, leadership development, advocacy skills, and community engagement. 1. Climate Science Participants will develop a strong understanding of: Climate systems Climate change drivers Local environmental impacts Global climate trends This foundation helps learners understand the science behind climate-related challenges. 2. Community-Led Climate Solutions Participants will learn how to: Identify local environmental challenges Develop community-based responses Apply the Participatory Action Research (PAR) toolkit Lead local climate initiatives The emphasis is on creating practical solutions tailored to community needs. 3. Climate Justice The course explores how climate change affects different communities unequally. Participants will examine: Social inequality Environmental justice Economic impacts Vulnerable populations Inclusive climate action Understanding climate justice enables participants to develop more equitable solutions. 4. Sustainable Development Participants will gain knowledge about: Community resilience Sustainable development principles Environmental sustainability Long-term climate adaptation strategies The course demonstrates how climate action contributes to broader sustainable development goals. 5. Climate Advocacy Effective communication is essential for creating change. Participants will learn how to: Communicate climate issues effectively Engage policymakers Mobilize communities Create persuasive climate messages Advocate through digital platforms These skills prepare participants to become effective climate communicators. 6. Participatory Approaches The course introduces practical methods for engaging communities. Topics include: Facilitating climate workshops Community participation Stakeholder engagement Collaborative decision-making Participatory leadership These approaches encourage inclusive climate action. 7. Leadership and Personal Development Participants will strengthen their leadership capacity by learning how to: Build confidence Develop leadership skills Inspire others Lead community initiatives Become effective youth climate champions Leadership development is integrated throughout the course. What Makes This Course Different? Several features distinguish this program from traditional online climate courses. Learn with an International Cohort Participants will join a diverse learning community consisting of young people from eight countries across Africa and the Middle East. This creates opportunities to: Exchange ideas Share experiences Learn from different contexts Build lasting regional connections Receive Coaching from Climate Ambassadors Every participant receives mentorship from DOT Climate Ambassadors. These mentors are former Climate Champions who provide: Practical guidance Coaching Feedback Motivation Support throughout the program Apply Learning to Real Community Challenges Rather than limiting learning to theory, participants are encouraged to apply new knowledge to actual climate issues affecting their communities. This practical approach enhances learning outcomes and creates meaningful local impact. Opportunity to Pitch Climate Ideas Outstanding participants may have the opportunity to: Develop their climate innovation ideas Pitch community climate solutions Become part of a future Climate Innovation Bootcamp This creates additional pathways for continued learning and innovation. Who Should Join? The course welcomes participants from diverse educational and professional backgrounds. It is particularly suitable for: Students Young professionals Community leaders Climate advocates Social entrepreneurs Volunteers Youth interested in environmental sustainability No previous experience in climate science or environmental work is required. Target Countries DOT especially encourages applications from young people aged 18–35 living in: Tanzania Kenya Ethiopia Rwanda Lebanon Jordan Malawi Zambia Youth from climate-vulnerable communities and individuals with limited access to formal climate education are especially encouraged to participate. Click HERE to read more. How to Join Interested applicants can participate by following these simple steps: Register for the upcoming cohort. Receive onboarding information and the official course start date. Complete the six-week virtual learning experience. Successfully finish all course requirements. Receive your completion certificate. Because each cohort includes dedicated coaching and mentorship, spaces are limited. Applicants are encouraged to register as early as possible. Why You Should Apply The Climate Leadership for Community Action Virtual Course provides much more than online lessons. Participants will gain: Practical climate leadership skills International networking opportunities Expert coaching Community engagement experience Climate advocacy skills Sustainable development knowledge Confidence to lead local initiatives Official certificate upon completion Opportunity to advance to future Climate Innovation Bootcamps For young people seeking to make a meaningful contribution to climate action while building valuable leadership skills, this program represents an outstanding opportunity. Click HERE to register. Deadline No specific application deadline has been announced. Since participation is cohort-based and spaces are limited, interested applicants are encouraged to register as early as possible to secure a place in Cohort 2 . Conclusion The Climate Leadership for Community Action Virtual Course 2026 offers young people across Africa and the Middle East an exceptional opportunity to become effective climate leaders through practical learning, international collaboration, and expert mentorship. Over six weeks, participants will build essential knowledge in climate science, climate justice, community engagement, advocacy, and sustainable development while working alongside peers committed to creating positive environmental change. With no prior experience required and an official certificate awarded upon successful completion, this program provides an accessible pathway for aspiring climate champions to develop real-world skills and contribute to resilient, sustainable communities. Those interested should register early, as available places are limited. Click HERE for more course opportunities. title: OECD Policy Analyst – Economist Job 2026 in France: Open-Ended International Policy Career in Social Economy and Social Innovation
summary: Are you an experienced economist, policy analyst, public policy expert, or social innovation professional looking to work with one of the world s leading international organisations? The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development ( OECD ) has opened applications for the Policy Analyst – Economist (Social Economy and Social Innovation) position within its Centre for Entrepreneurship, SMEs, Regions and Cities (CFE) in Paris, France. This is an exceptional opportunity for experienced professionals who are passionate about evidence-based policymaking, economic development, social entrepreneurship, labour markets, employment creation, and social innovation. Successful applicants will join an international multidisciplinary team working with governments across OECD member and partner countries to develop policies that promote inclusive economic growth, sustainable employment, entrepreneurship, and stronger communities. The position offers an open-ended appointment , a highly competitive salary starting at EUR 6,975.54 per month , international exposure, opportunities to influence global public policy, and the chance to work with leading experts from around the world. If you have extensive experience in economics, policy analysis, social economy research, or international development, this could be your next major career opportunity. About the OECD The Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) is an international organisation comprising 38 member countries that work together to build better policies for better lives. For decades, the OECD has supported governments by providing: Evidence-based policy recommendations High-quality research and economic analysis International standards Comparative statistics Policy benchmarking International cooperation Best-practice sharing Its work spans numerous policy areas including: Economic growth Employment Education Innovation Taxation Climate Entrepreneurship Digital transformation Artificial Intelligence Regional development Public governance Social inclusion The OECD serves as one of the world s most influential policy organisations, helping governments design solutions to today s most pressing economic and social challenges. About the Centre for Entrepreneurship, SMEs, Regions and Cities (CFE) The successful candidate will join the OECD s Centre for Entrepreneurship, SMEs, Regions and Cities (CFE). The CFE supports governments by providing research, policy advice, statistics, and recommendations aimed at strengthening: Entrepreneurship Small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) Regional development Cities Tourism Local employment Sustainable economic growth Its mission is to help countries create stronger local economies while promoting innovation, competitiveness, and inclusive growth. About the Local Employment and Social Innovation Division (LESI) Within CFE, the successful applicant will work in the Local Employment and Social Innovation Division (LESI). The division focuses on helping countries strengthen: Local economic development Job creation Employment policies Workforce development Skills strategies Social inclusion Place-based development Social innovation Social entrepreneurship The division conducts policy-oriented research in collaboration with governments, international organisations, researchers, and local stakeholders. About the Position The OECD is recruiting an experienced Policy Analyst – Economist to support research and policy development on: Social economy Social entrepreneurship Social innovation The selected candidate will conduct analytical research examining how social economy organisations contribute to: Employment creation Skills development Economic growth Community resilience Public policy implementation Sustainable development Research will explore multiple sectors including: Care services Housing Energy Retail Labour market programmes The successful applicant will also examine important policy framework conditions including: Taxation Legal frameworks Market access Finance Impact measurement Data systems Evidence generation Digital transformation Artificial Intelligence Productivity Competitiveness The position reports directly to the Head of the Social Economy and Innovation Unit . Key Responsibilities The selected Policy Analyst/Economist will undertake a broad range of strategic, analytical, research, and project management responsibilities. Research and Policy Analysis Responsibilities include: Designing and leading high-impact research projects Conducting policy-oriented research Preparing international comparative analyses Developing case studies Producing evidence-based policy recommendations Writing thematic reports Drafting policy papers Preparing analytical publications Writing blogs and policy briefs Monitoring emerging international policy trends Reviewing international evidence Analysing social economy data Producing recommendations for policymakers Strategic Leadership The successful candidate will also: Provide strategic advice to OECD committees Contribute to Programme of Work and Budget implementation Lead country-level projects Coordinate peer-learning activities Support policy capacity-building initiatives Manage consultants and junior analysts Collaborate across OECD directorates Oversee workshops and meetings Prepare speeches and presentations Draft communication materials Project Management Responsibilities also include: Delivering projects on time Managing project outputs Supporting fundraising initiatives Preparing project proposals Supervising interns and consultants Building relationships with donors Strengthening project partnerships International Representation The selected candidate will represent the OECD by: Participating in international conferences Engaging with government officials Working with ministries Collaborating with international organisations Organising webinars Delivering workshops Supporting online knowledge platforms Promoting OECD research internationally What Does the OECD Offer? Successful applicants will receive an attractive international employment package that includes: Open-ended employment contract Monthly salary starting at EUR 6,975.54 Additional allowances (subject to eligibility) Salary exempt from French income tax Opportunity to work in Paris, France International working environment Collaboration with leading economists and policymakers Career growth within the OECD Exposure to international public policy Opportunities to lead global research initiatives Professional development opportunities Participation in multidisciplinary international projects The OECD also notes that appointments may be made at a lower grade depending on the successful candidate s qualifications and professional experience. Who Can Apply? Applicants should possess a strong combination of academic qualifications, professional experience, policy expertise, and analytical skills. Academic Requirements Applicants should have: An advanced university degree in: Economics Public Policy Social Sciences Or another closely related discipline Professional Experience Candidates should have: At least three years of relevant professional experience Preferably five or more years of experience Experience should have been gained in organisations such as: International organisations National governments Regional governments Universities Research institutes Policy institutions Knowledge and Skills Required Successful candidates should demonstrate: Strong understanding of OECD policy work Excellent analytical skills Policy research expertise Report writing experience Evidence-based policy development Stakeholder engagement experience Strong presentation skills Excellent communication abilities Ability to communicate with policymakers Experience producing policy recommendations Applicants should also possess: Strong Microsoft Office skills Core Competencies Applicants are expected to demonstrate OECD core competencies including: Vision and Strategy Ethics and Integrity Collaboration Innovation Results Orientation People Management Language Requirements Applicants must have: Fluency in English or French Additionally: Knowledge of the second OECD official language is required, with a commitment to reach a good working level. Additional languages are considered an advantage. Salary The starting monthly salary is: EUR 6,975.54 Additional allowances may be provided depending on eligibility. The salary is exempt from French income tax. Contract Type The selected applicant will receive: Open-ended appointment Full-time employment No foreseen contract end date Selection Process Retained candidates may be invited to participate in: Video-recorded interview Job-based assessment Panel interview The OECD may also use this recruitment process to establish a pool of candidates for future vacancies. Equal Opportunity Employer The OECD is committed to diversity and inclusion. Applications are welcomed from qualified candidates regardless of: Race Ethnic origin Gender Sexual orientation Beliefs Disability status However, applicants must meet the nationality requirements for OECD member countries. Application Deadline Applications must be submitted no later than 27 August 2026 (midnight Paris time). Interested applicants are encouraged to submit their applications as early as possible. APPLY HERE Why You Should Apply Working at the OECD offers far more than a traditional policy role. It provides the opportunity to influence international policymaking while collaborating with experts from governments, academia, and international organisations. Whether your expertise lies in economics, social innovation, labour markets, entrepreneurship, public policy, or international development, this role offers a unique platform to shape policies that improve lives globally. Beyond the attractive salary and permanent contract, successful candidates will gain invaluable experience working on internationally recognised research, advising governments, and contributing to policies that promote inclusive growth, employment creation, and sustainable development. If you are passionate about using research and evidence to solve global economic and social challenges, this OECD Policy Analyst – Economist position represents an outstanding career opportunity. Visit HERE for more information about the OECD Policy Analyst – Economist Job 2026 in France Conclusion The OECD Policy Analyst – Economist (Social Economy and Social Innovation) position is an outstanding opportunity for experienced professionals seeking to contribute to global policy development while advancing their careers within one of the world s most respected international organisations. With an open-ended appointment, a competitive salary starting at EUR 6,975.54 per month, international exposure, and the chance to influence public policy across OECD member and partner countries, this role provides an exceptional platform for economists, policy analysts, researchers, and social innovation experts. Interested candidates should ensure they meet the academic, professional, language, and nationality requirements and submit their applications before 27 August 2026 . title: European Commission Blue Book Traineeship Programme 2027: The Complete Guide to Europe’s Most Prestigious Paid Traineeship in EU Policymaking and Administration
summary: The European Commission s Blue Book Traineeship Programme is one of the most coveted and internationally recognized paid traineeship opportunities available to graduates anywhere in the world. Offered twice a year by the European Commission the executive arm of the European Union this five-month traineeship places successful candidates at the heart of EU policymaking, administration, and institutional operations, providing unparalleled exposure to how one of the world s most consequential governing bodies functions from the inside. Registration for the March 2027 session is now open, and the deadline to apply is 4 September 2026 at 10:00 AM Brussels time. This article provides a comprehensive, detailed, and fully up-to-date guide to everything you need to know about the Blue Book Traineeship Programme its three strands, what it offers, the financial package, working conditions, eligibility, the application process, career pathways, and practical information for prospective applicants. What Is the Blue Book Traineeship Programme? The Blue Book Traineeship Programme is a structured, five-month paid traineeship offered by the European Commission twice a year. It is managed by the Directorate-General for Human Resources and Security and provides graduates with hands-on experience working within one of the most complex and influential multilateral institutions in the world. Trainees are placed across a wide range of departments, bodies, and entities connected to the European Commission, including: European Commission Directorate-Generals (DGs) Executive agencies of the European Commission Decentralised agencies and EU bodies Commissioners Cabinets The European External Action Service (EEAS) The programme offers trainees a genuinely immersive professional experience in a multicultural and multilingual environment, working alongside experienced EU civil servants on real policy work, administrative tasks, research, communications, translation, and more. The five core pillars of what the Blue Book Traineeship offers are: Hands-on experience in EU policymaking and administration Professional skills development across a wide range of competencies Intercultural awareness and personal growth through engagement with colleagues from across the EU and beyond Language development in a multilingual working environment A career launchpad with direct pathways into EU institutions and agencies Learn More The Three Traineeship Strands One of the most important things to understand about the Blue Book Traineeship Programme is that it is organized into three distinct strands, each designed for a different graduate profile. Applicants may apply to only one strand per session, so it is essential to carefully review the requirements of each before submitting an application. 1. Administrative Traineeship for University Graduates (ADMIN UG) The Administrative Traineeship for University Graduates is the most widely known and largest strand of the Blue Book programme. It is designed for individuals who have completed a university-level degree (bachelor s, master s, or equivalent) and who wish to gain experience in EU policy, administration, research, communications, economics, law, public affairs, or any of the many other fields represented within the European Commission s work. Trainees placed under the ADMIN UG strand work in Commission Directorate-Generals, executive agencies, Commissioners Cabinets, the European External Action Service, and other EU bodies, contributing to the real work of their assigned unit under the supervision of an adviser. Detailed eligibility criteria for the ADMIN UG strand are available in the dedicated section of the Blue Book Traineeship website. Prospective applicants are strongly encouraged to review these criteria carefully, as requirements may include minimum education levels, language proficiency, nationality conditions, and other factors. 2. Administrative Traineeship for VET Graduates (ADMIN VET) The Administrative Traineeship for Vocational Education and Training Graduates is a newer strand of the Blue Book programme and represents a significant expansion of access to EU institutional experience. This strand is specifically designed for individuals who have completed a vocational education and training (VET) qualification and who may not hold a traditional university degree. The introduction of the ADMIN VET strand reflects the European Commission s commitment to equal opportunities and inclusive access to EU career pathways. It acknowledges that vocational training is a valuable and legitimate educational route, and that VET graduates bring practical, hands-on skills that are genuinely valuable within the Commission s operations. As with the ADMIN UG strand, detailed eligibility criteria for the ADMIN VET traineeship are available on the Blue Book website and should be reviewed carefully before applying. 3. Translation Traineeship (TRA) The Translation Traineeship is designed for graduates with strong language skills and an interest in EU translation, linguistics, or multilingual communication. The European Commission operates in 24 official EU languages, making translation one of its most essential functions. The Translation Traineeship places candidates within the Directorate-General for Translation or related language services, where they contribute to the translation of official EU documents, policy texts, legal materials, communications, and other content. Applicants to the TRA strand should have a strong command of multiple EU languages, including a deep proficiency in at least one language other than their mother tongue. Full eligibility criteria are available on the dedicated Translation Traineeship section of the Blue Book website. Sessions and Calendar The Blue Book Traineeship Programme is offered twice a year, with two main sessions: A March session (typically running from March to July) An October session (typically running from October to February) Registration for the March 2027 session is currently open. The deadline to apply for the March 2027 session is 4 September 2026 at 10:00 AM Brussels time. This is a firm deadline, and late applications will not be accepted. Applicants who are not selected in one session are welcome to apply again in future sessions. There is no limit on the number of times a candidate may apply. Financial Package: Monthly Grants, Allowances, and Benefits The Blue Book Traineeship is a paid programme. Trainees receive a monthly living allowance, the amount of which is adjusted using a correction coefficient based on the cost of living in the country or city of assignment. Monthly Living Grant by Country (March 2027 Session Indicative Figures) The following indicative basic grants, adjusted by correction coefficient, apply for the March 2027 session: Austria: 1,680 euros Belgium: 1,572 euros Bulgaria: 1,047 euros Croatia: 1,273 euros Cyprus: 1,267 euros Czech Republic: 1,443 euros Denmark: 2,081 euros Estonia: 1,552 euros Finland: 1,759 euros France: 1,814 euros Germany: 1,638 euros Greece: 1,396 euros Hungary: 1,232 euros Ireland: 2,067 euros Italy: 1,390 euros Latvia: 1,366 euros Lithuania: 1,412 euros Luxembourg: 1,572 euros Malta: 1,487 euros Netherlands: 1,803 euros Poland: 1,314 euros Portugal: 1,470 euros Romania: 1,179 euros Slovakia: 1,376 euros Slovenia: 1,396 euros Spain: 1,471 euros Sweden: 1,894 euros Additional Allowances Subject to budget availability, trainees may also receive: A general travel allowance of 275 euros (one-time) A meal and public transport allowance equivalent to 5 percent of the adjusted monthly grant (available in most countries) An accommodation allowance equivalent to 20 percent of the adjusted monthly grant (available in Luxembourg only, for the March 2027 session) Disability Supplementary Allowance Upon submission of proof of a recognized disability, issued by a competent national authority, trainees with disabilities are eligible for a supplementary allowance of up to 50 percent of the adjusted monthly grant. This provision reflects the European Commission s commitment to equal opportunity and inclusion. What Is Not Covered It is important to note that the European Commission does not cover accommodation expenses for trainees. Trainees placed in Brussels, for example, can expect to pay on average between 700 and 1,000 euros per month for accommodation in the form of a flat, studio, or shared apartment. Trainees should also be prepared to pay one or two months rent as a security deposit when arranging housing. Learn More Working Conditions Working Hours Commission employees, including trainees, work 40 hours per week, at 8 hours per day. Official working hours span from 8:00 AM to 7:00 PM, with a minimum lunch break of 20 minutes. The actual hours a trainee works will depend on their specific unit and workload, and trainees should clarify expected working patterns with their adviser upon arrival. Telework Trainees may be authorized to telework from a location other than their place of assignment for a maximum of 5 days during a 5-month traineeship. Teleworking from a country other than the place of assignment is generally not authorized. Leave and Holidays Leave requests must respect the needs of the assigned unit. Trainees should discuss and agree on leave arrangements with their adviser. It is advisable to plan any pre-booked holidays in consultation with the adviser before the traineeship begins. Missions Participation in official missions is only possible in exceptional cases, requiring authorization from the Director General of the relevant Directorate-General or Service. Such missions must be technical in nature and not of a representative character. Parallel Remunerated Activity As a general principle, trainees are strongly advised to suspend any other remunerated activity during the traineeship period. The traineeship is designed as a full-time experience, and trainees are encouraged to devote as much time as possible to it. However, the rules around parallel remunerated activities are nuanced, and trainees with specific questions should contact the Traineeships Office for guidance. Dress Code The European Commission maintains a professional working environment. Trainees are expected to dress appropriately for a professional institutional setting. Learn More Insurance All Blue Book trainees are covered by an accident and health insurance scheme provided by the European Commission for the duration of their traineeship. Trainees may also choose to maintain their own personal health insurance alongside the Commission s scheme. If doing so, trainees should verify that their own insurance is valid for the entire duration of the traineeship and across the whole of Europe. The Commission s insurance coverage can also be extended for up to 12 months immediately after the end of the traineeship, without interruption, under different conditions and costs from the in-traineeship cover. It is important to note that trainees are not insured against theft of personal property, either inside or outside Commission premises. Trainees are fully responsible for their own possessions at all times. Career Pathways After the Blue Book Traineeship Completing the Blue Book Traineeship can open significant doors within the EU institutional landscape. There are several career pathway options available to former trainees: Commission Junior Professionals Programme (JPP) The Junior Professionals Programme (JPP) is a European Commission initiative designed to better integrate junior staff into EU civil service. It includes a competitive selection process based on an on-the-job assessment and pre-selection by Commission Directorate-Generals or Executive Agencies. Blue Book trainees from Commission DGs and Executive Agencies who fulfil the eligibility criteria including having a maximum of three years of professional experience and being employed by the Commission or an Executive Agency at the time of the call are eligible to apply for the JPP. It is important to note that trainees from decentralized agencies and other EU institutions are not eligible for the JPP, as it is specifically limited to Commission DGs and Executive Agencies. Learn More EU Careers via EPSO All Blue Book trainees, regardless of where they are placed, can explore broader EU career opportunities through EU Careers, the European Personnel Selection Office (EPSO), which manages open competitions for permanent positions across EU institutions. Temporary Jobs at the European Commission Additional career opportunities in the form of temporary employment at the European Commission are also available and can be explored through the Commission s official recruitment channels. Practical Information for Applicants Applying to Multiple Strands or Sessions Applicants may only apply to one traineeship strand per session. However, they may simultaneously apply to traineeship schemes at other EU institutions. If offered a placement at more than one EU institution, applicants must make a choice, and they should be aware that having already completed more than 42 calendar days at any other European institution, EU body, EU agency, or EU delegation will affect eligibility. Withdrawing or Cancelling an Application Applicants may withdraw their application by informing the Traineeships Office in writing. A withdrawal excludes the candidate from any further stages of that particular application process, but they may re-apply in a future session by submitting a new application with all supporting documents. Application Documents Applicants should note that supporting documents submitted through the application portal are not stored in the system after submission. Candidates should retain their own copies of all supporting documents. Bank Accounts Trainees placed in Belgium should be aware that most Belgian banks offer free bank accounts to individuals under the age of 26. Trainees should check with their chosen bank to confirm whether this applies to them. Business Cards Blue Book trainees are not authorized to use business cards that associate them with their Directorate-General or their European Commission email address. Learn More Application Deadline The deadline to apply for the March 2027 session of the Blue Book Traineeship Programme is 4 September 2026 at 10:00 AM Brussels time . Applications submitted after this deadline will not be considered. Prospective applicants are strongly encouraged to begin their applications early to ensure they have sufficient time to gather all required supporting documents and review the detailed eligibility criteria for their chosen strand. Applications must be submitted through the official Blue Book Traineeship online application portal, accessible via the Blue Book Traineeship Programme website. APPLY HERE Conclusion The European Commission Blue Book Traineeship Programme stands as one of the most prestigious, impactful, and professionally enriching paid traineeship opportunities available to graduates in Europe and beyond. With three distinct strands the Administrative Traineeship for University Graduates, the Administrative Traineeship for VET Graduates, and the Translation Traineeship the programme is genuinely inclusive and accessible to a diverse range of educational backgrounds and professional profiles. For five months, selected trainees work at the very heart of EU institutional life, gaining hands-on exposure to EU policymaking, administration, translation, policy research, and international relations, all while earning a monthly living allowance adjusted to the cost of living in their country of assignment, and benefiting from health and accident insurance, a travel allowance, and potential meal and transport support. With the March 2027 session now open and the application deadline set for 4 September 2026 at 10:00 AM Brussels time, the window to apply is open but will close quickly. Whether you are a university graduate, a VET graduate, or a language specialist with a passion for EU affairs, this is an opportunity to launch or accelerate your career in European and international affairs from one of the most important institutional environments in the world. Review the eligibility criteria for your chosen strand, prepare your supporting documents carefully, and submit your application well before the deadline. For more information and to apply, visit the official Blue Book Traineeship Programme website managed by the European Commission s Directorate-General for Human Resources and Security. Discover more global Traineeship and European opportunities on OFY. title: Fall 2026 Fulcrum Journalism Fellowship Opens Applications for U.S. Journalism Students with $1,000 Stipend
summary: Applications are now open for the Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship, a paid journalism training and mentorship opportunity created for undergraduate and graduate journalism students studying in the United States. The fellowship is designed to help emerging reporters develop stronger skills in solutions-focused journalism, narrative complexity, civic reporting, climate solutions journalism, and community-centered storytelling. Selected fellows will receive practical training, editorial mentorship, opportunities to produce and publish original journalism, and a $1,000 stipend after successfully completing the program. The Fall 2026 cohort will run from September 14 to October 20, 2026. Applications must be submitted by August 3, 2026. The official announcement does not specify a deadline time or timezone, so applicants should avoid waiting until the final day to submit their materials. Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship at a Glance Program name: Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship Organizing publication: The Fulcrum Program initiative: The Fulcrum NextGen Fellowship period: September 14 to October 20, 2026 Application deadline: August 3, 2026 Financial support: $1,000 stipend upon completion Eligible academic levels: Undergraduate and graduate students Required field: Journalism Geographical eligibility: Students studying in the United States Academic requirement: Good academic standing Main training areas: Solutions journalism, narrative complexity, civic storytelling, community-centered reporting and climate solutions journalism Application method: Submission by email Publication opportunities: Original reporting may be published by The Fulcrum and the Latino News Network What Is the Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship? The Fulcrum Fellowship is a journalism development program intended to support and train emerging reporters who want to move beyond simplistic, divisive or highly polarized news narratives. Rather than teaching fellows to report only on problems, conflict or institutional failures, the program introduces them to approaches that examine how individuals, communities and organizations are responding to public challenges. The purpose is not to avoid difficult subjects, but to report on those subjects with greater depth, evidence, context and human understanding. The Fall 2026 program will focus particularly on solutions-oriented reporting and the practice of “complicating the narrative.” This approach encourages reporters to look beyond two-sided political or social arguments and examine the experiences, motivations, contradictions and perspectives that are often missing from conventional reporting. Through these methods, fellows will learn how to produce journalism that presents communities as active participants in public life rather than merely as subjects affected by problems. The fellowship is part of The Fulcrum’s broader NextGen initiative, which seeks to increase opportunities for student journalists and strengthen youth participation in reporting about democracy, public policy, civic engagement and community renewal. First Fall Cohort of the Fulcrum Fellowship The 2026 program represents the first time that The Fulcrum is extending its fellowship program into the fall season. The expansion has been made possible through financial support from the Hortencia Zavala Foundation. The addition of a fall cohort creates another entry point for journalism students who may not have been able to participate in previous program cycles. The fall fellowship builds upon the experience of earlier cohorts and The Fulcrum’s ongoing work with student journalists. Previous initiatives have focused on preparing young reporters to cover complex social and political issues while avoiding narratives that reinforce polarization or reduce communities to stereotypes. By expanding the program, The Fulcrum aims to support more emerging journalists and strengthen the pipeline of reporters capable of producing responsible, constructive and community-focused public-interest journalism. Fellowship Dates and Program Duration The Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship will take place from: September 14 to October 20, 2026 This means the program will operate for approximately six weeks. The announcement does not provide a detailed weekly schedule, session timetable or expected number of hours that fellows must contribute. Applicants with university classes, employment or other obligations should request clarification from the organizers if scheduling information is necessary before they apply. The relatively concentrated program period is expected to combine training, mentorship, reporting and the preparation of an original journalistic story. Main Objectives of the Fellowship The Fall Fulcrum Fellowship is intended to help participants: Develop practical skills in solutions-focused journalism. Understand how to report on complex political, civic and social issues without relying on oversimplified narratives. Produce original, evidence-based and community-centered reporting. Improve interviewing, research, writing and narrative-development skills. Explore how communities are responding to challenges affecting democracy and civic life. Receive professional guidance from experienced editors and journalism trainers. Build a stronger portfolio through potential publication. Develop experience in climate solutions journalism. Report more effectively across political, cultural and social differences. Prepare for future careers in newsrooms, nonprofit media, public-interest journalism and civic communication. Training in Solutions Journalism Solutions journalism is one of the central components of the fellowship. Under this approach, journalists investigate responses to social, environmental, political or economic problems. Reporting does not simply celebrate positive initiatives or promote organizations. Instead, it examines how a response works, what evidence supports it, what limitations it faces and whether lessons can be drawn from it. Fellows will be encouraged to look beyond descriptions of problems and investigate questions such as: Who is responding to the problem? What strategy or intervention is being used? What evidence shows whether the response is working? What challenges or limitations remain? Can the response provide lessons for other communities? Which voices are usually excluded from reporting on the issue? How can the story avoid exaggeration while still identifying meaningful progress? This training can help journalism students produce stories that are more informative and useful to communities while maintaining professional standards of verification and independence. Learning to “Complicate the Narrative” The program will also train participants in techniques commonly described as “complicating the narrative.” Traditional reporting frequently presents difficult public issues as conflicts between two opposing sides. Although this format can make a story appear simple, it may overlook important perspectives, internal disagreements, personal experiences and areas of common ground. Complicating the narrative involves asking deeper questions and recognizing that people’s beliefs and experiences do not always fit neatly into fixed political, cultural or social categories. For emerging journalists, this can involve: Interviewing people with different experiences and perspectives. Avoiding assumptions about communities or political groups. Exploring why individuals hold particular views. Identifying contradictions and changes in people’s opinions. Examining areas of agreement that may exist alongside disagreement. Including community voices that are often ignored. Reporting on people as individuals rather than political labels. Avoiding language that unnecessarily intensifies division. These skills can be particularly valuable when covering elections, public policy, immigration, education, climate change, local government, social inequality and other sensitive issues. Climate Solutions Journalism Component The Fall 2026 fellowship will include a climate solutions journalism component supported through sponsorship from the Solutions Journalism Network. Participants will receive training in reporting on responses to climate-related challenges and will produce an original story applying the methods introduced during the program. Climate journalism often focuses on disasters, environmental damage and scientific warnings. These issues remain essential, but solutions-focused reporting can also investigate how communities, institutions, researchers, businesses or governments are attempting to address climate risks. Potential reporting areas may include local climate adaptation, clean energy, sustainable transportation, water management, environmental justice, disaster preparedness, conservation, resilient infrastructure or community-led environmental initiatives. The fellowship announcement does not assign a specific climate topic in advance. Participants should therefore be prepared to identify, research and report an appropriate story in consultation with the program’s editors and mentors. Mentorship and Editorial Support Fellows will receive mentorship from members of the publication’s editorial team. Editorial mentorship is an important element of the program because it gives students an opportunity to develop their work with guidance from professionals who understand reporting, story development, verification, structure and publication standards. Depending on the needs of each story, mentorship may help fellows strengthen areas such as: Developing a clear and reportable story idea. Identifying relevant sources. Preparing effective interview questions. Examining evidence behind proposed solutions. Organizing complex information. Improving narrative flow. Distinguishing reporting from advocacy. Revising drafts in response to editorial feedback. Producing publication-ready journalism. The fellowship is led by Hugo Balta, publisher of the Latino News Network and executive editor of The Fulcrum. He is also an accredited solutions-journalism trainer with the Solutions Journalism Network and has previously served twice as president of the National Association of Hispanic Journalists. Opportunity to Publish Original Reporting Participants will have opportunities to produce and publish original journalism during the fellowship. The announcement states that fellows may have their work published through The Fulcrum. Their reporting may also appear on the Latino News Network, potentially allowing their stories to reach audiences interested in democracy, civic participation, public policy and community renewal. Publication can provide substantial professional value for journalism students. A published, professionally edited article can become part of a fellow’s reporting portfolio and may support future applications for internships, newsroom positions, graduate programs or additional fellowships. Applicants should understand that the opportunity is based on producing work that meets editorial and program requirements. The announcement does not guarantee that every draft will automatically be published without review or revision. $1,000 Fellowship Stipend Each fellow who completes the Fall 2026 program will receive a $1,000 stipend . The wording of the announcement indicates that payment is connected to completion of the fellowship. Participants should therefore be prepared to attend the required sessions, participate in the training process, work with mentors and complete the expected reporting assignment. The announcement does not provide further information about: The exact stipend payment date. Whether payment will be made in one installment. Tax treatment of the stipend. Whether any additional reporting expenses will be reimbursed. Whether fellows must provide specific tax or payment documentation. Applicants who require clarification regarding these financial arrangements should contact the fellowship organizers directly before accepting an offer. Who Is Eligible to Apply? The fellowship is open to: Undergraduate journalism students studying in the United States. Graduate journalism students studying in the United States. Students who are in good academic standing. The announcement does not mention a minimum grade-point average, age limit, graduation year, citizenship requirement or racial or ethnic restriction. It also does not clarify whether applicants must be enrolled in a degree formally titled “Journalism” or whether students in closely related fields such as communications, media studies or digital media will be considered. Students in related academic programs should contact the organizers for confirmation rather than assuming that they meet the field-of-study requirement. Application Documents Required Applicants must prepare the following four materials: Current Résumé The résumé should provide an updated overview of the applicant’s education, journalism experience, employment, internships, campus activities, volunteer work, technical abilities and relevant achievements. Students may include experience such as: Reporting for a university newspaper. Producing content for radio, television or digital media. Freelance writing. Podcasting. Documentary or multimedia production. Photography or video journalism. Community reporting. Journalism-related coursework. Research projects. Civic or public-policy engagement. Applicants should ensure that their contact information, educational status and experience are accurate and current. Examples of Published or Academic Work Applicants must submit examples of their work. These may include published journalism or relevant academic assignments. Suitable examples could include: News articles. Feature stories. Investigative reports. Multimedia journalism. Audio or video reports. University newspaper contributions. Class reporting assignments. Research-based academic writing. Community or civic reporting. Applicants should select samples that demonstrate clear writing, research ability, accuracy, curiosity and an understanding of journalistic storytelling. The announcement does not specify the number, length or preferred format of the samples. Applicants should therefore submit a focused selection of their strongest and most relevant work rather than sending an excessive number of documents. Cover Letter A cover letter must explain how participating in the fellowship would support the applicant’s professional and journalistic growth. A strong letter should directly address: The applicant’s interest in journalism. Relevant academic or reporting experience. Interest in solutions-focused reporting. Professional skills the applicant hopes to develop. Interest in democracy, civic engagement, public policy, community reporting or climate solutions. How mentorship and publication experience would support future goals. What perspective or experience the applicant would contribute to the cohort. The cover letter should be tailored specifically to the Fall Fulcrum Fellowship rather than using a generic internship or employment letter. One Letter of Recommendation Applicants must provide one recommendation letter. The announcement does not state who must write the recommendation. A suitable recommender may therefore be a journalism professor, academic adviser, editor, newsroom supervisor or another individual familiar with the applicant’s academic work, reporting ability and professional conduct. Applicants should approach their recommender early and provide sufficient time for the letter to be completed before the August 3 deadline. How to Apply for the Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship There is no separate online application form mentioned in the announcement. Applications must be submitted by email. Click HERE . Follow these steps: Prepare an updated résumé. Select examples of published or academic work. Write a tailored cover letter explaining how the fellowship will support your growth. Obtain one letter of recommendation. Review all documents for accuracy and completeness. Attach the materials to an email. Use the required subject line: Fall Fulcrum Fellowship application Send the complete application to newsroom@fulcrum.us Submit the application no later than August 3, 2026. Applicants should confirm that all attachments open correctly and use clearly identifiable filenames, preferably including the applicant’s name and the document type. For example: Firstname-Lastname-Resume.pdf Firstname-Lastname-Cover-Letter.pdf Firstname-Lastname-Writing-Samples.pdf Firstname-Lastname-Recommendation.pdf All application requirements and the required email subject line should be followed exactly. Important Dates Application Deadline August 3, 2026 No submission time or timezone is included in the announcement. Early submission is therefore strongly recommended. Fellowship Start Date September 14, 2026 Fellowship End Date October 20, 2026 Applicants should ensure that they will be available throughout the program period before submitting an application. Why Journalism Students Should Consider Applying The Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship offers more than a financial stipend. It provides emerging journalists with an opportunity to develop reporting skills that are increasingly relevant in a divided and rapidly changing media environment. Participants can benefit from: Professional journalism training. Direct editorial mentorship. Experience producing an original reported story. Exposure to solutions journalism methods. Training in climate solutions reporting. Opportunities for publication. Portfolio development. Experience covering civic and community issues. A stronger understanding of narrative complexity. A $1,000 completion stipend. The program may be especially valuable for students who want to report on democracy, public policy, climate challenges, civic participation and local community responses while avoiding superficial or unnecessarily polarized narratives. Tips for Preparing a Competitive Application Applicants should consider the following practical measures: Follow every instruction carefully. Missing documents or using an incorrect email subject line may weaken an otherwise strong application. Select relevant work samples. Submit pieces that demonstrate reporting, research, interviewing or evidence-based storytelling. Write a specific cover letter. Explain precisely how the fellowship connects to your experience and future journalism goals. Demonstrate curiosity about communities. The fellowship emphasizes human-centered and community-focused reporting. Show openness to editorial feedback. Mentorship and revision are central to professional journalism development. Mention relevant interests. Experience or interest in civic affairs, democracy, public policy, climate reporting or solutions journalism can strengthen the application when presented honestly. Contact a recommender immediately. Recommendation letters frequently require more time than applicants expect. Proofread the complete submission. Check names, dates, links, attachments, formatting and contact details. Apply before the deadline. The official announcement specifically encourages interested students to apply early. Conclusion The Fall 2026 Fulcrum Fellowship is a valuable paid opportunity for undergraduate and graduate journalism students in the United States who want to strengthen their reporting, writing and storytelling abilities. Through training in solutions journalism, narrative complexity, climate reporting and community-centered storytelling, participants will gain practical tools for covering difficult public issues with greater evidence, depth and accuracy. Fellows will also receive professional mentorship, produce original reporting, gain potential publication exposure through The Fulcrum and the Latino News Network, and receive a $1,000 stipend after completing the program. Eligible journalism students should begin preparing their résumé, work samples, cover letter and recommendation letter as soon as possible. Complete applications must be emailed to newsroom@fulcrum.us with the subject line “Fall Fulcrum Fellowship application” by August 3, 2026 . For more global opportunities, click HERE . title: Senior Policy Manager at J-PAL Europe in Zurich, Switzerland | Work with Nobel Laureates on Evidence-Based Public Policy
summary: Are you an experienced public policy professional passionate about transforming rigorous research into real-world policy impact? The Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab (J-PAL) Europe is seeking a Senior Policy Manager to join its team in Zurich, Switzerland . This prestigious opportunity allows the successful candidate to work directly with J-PAL co-founders and Nobel Prize-winning economists Professor Abhijit Banerjee and Professor Esther Duflo , supporting their global engagement with governments, multilateral organizations, philanthropic foundations, and development partners. The role is ideal for experienced professionals who have built strong careers in public policy, international development, economics, or related sectors and are eager to influence evidence-informed policymaking across Europe and beyond. About J-PAL Europe The Abdul Latif Jameel Poverty Action Lab (J-PAL) is a globally recognized research center dedicated to reducing poverty through evidence-based policymaking. Anchored by a network of more than 2,100 affiliated researchers at universities around the world, J-PAL works to ensure that public policies and social programs are informed by rigorous scientific research. J-PAL Europe is one of the organization s seven regional offices and is hosted by the Paris School of Economics . The office leads initiatives across Europe to promote the use of high-quality evidence in policymaking while collaborating with governments, international organizations, and development agencies. About the Position The Senior Policy Manager will be based in Zurich, Switzerland , and will work directly with Professors Abhijit Banerjee and Esther Duflo . Reporting jointly to the Global Executive Directors of J-PAL and the Executive Director of J-PAL Europe, the successful candidate will oversee strategic partnerships, manage high-level external engagements, support fundraising activities, and help translate academic research into accessible policy recommendations. Earliest Start Date: 1 September 2026 Latest Start Date: 1 September 2026 Key Responsibilities The successful candidate will play a central role in advancing J-PAL s mission through several core responsibilities. Strategic Partnership Management Responsibilities include: Managing partnership and collaboration requests directed to J-PAL s founders Reviewing and prioritizing incoming opportunities Assessing strategic alignment of partnership requests Building relationships with: European governments Foundations Development agencies Multilateral organizations Supporting long-term partnerships with European institutions Representing J-PAL during conferences and senior stakeholder meetings Policy Communication and Knowledge Dissemination The Senior Policy Manager will: Prepare policy briefs Write evidence summaries Develop speeches and opinion articles Produce blogs and communication materials Translate complex economic research into accessible policy messages Ensure scientific accuracy across communication outputs Support dissemination activities across the global J-PAL network Fundraising and Strategic Development Key duties include: Identifying funding opportunities Developing grant proposals Preparing concept notes Building relationships with current and prospective donors Supporting the strategic growth of J-PAL Europe s policy portfolio Coordination and Leadership The role also involves: Coordinating with J-PAL Europe and Global leadership Working closely with regional offices and affiliated researchers Managing external engagement activities Supporting policy training initiatives Contributing to organizational priorities Undertaking regular international travel to locations including: Zurich Paris Cambridge Other partner countries Professional Development Opportunities J-PAL invests heavily in staff development. Successful candidates will benefit from: Learn Free access to MIT s MicroMasters in Data, Economics, and Development Policy (DEDP) Continuous professional learning Collaboration with leading economists Engage Work directly with Nobel Prize-winning researchers Interact with policymakers worldwide Help shape evidence-based policies Travel Attend international conferences Meet government officials Collaborate with donor organizations Participate in policy events throughout Europe Grow Receive mentorship Share best practices across J-PAL s international network Build expertise in evidence-informed policymaking Eligibility Criteria Applicants should possess: Education Master s degree in: Public Policy Economics International Development Or another closely related discipline Professional Experience Candidates must have: At least 8 years of relevant professional experience Experience working with governments, multilateral organizations, foundations, or development agencies Demonstrated fundraising experience Experience writing successful grant proposals Excellent stakeholder management skills Technical Knowledge Applicants should have: Familiarity with randomized impact evaluations Understanding of evidence-informed policymaking Ability to communicate research findings clearly Knowledge of climate or environmental impact evaluations (desirable) Language Requirements Required: Fluency in English Preferred: Professional fluency in German Professional fluency in French Knowledge of both German and French will be a significant advantage. Desired Skills J-PAL seeks professionals who demonstrate: Exceptional writing ability Strong analytical thinking Strategic decision-making Excellent organizational skills Ability to manage multiple priorities Strong interpersonal communication Experience working across different countries and cultures Leadership and mentoring abilities Passion for evidence-based policymaking Work Authorization Applicants must already have authorization to work in Switzerland . Visa sponsorship is not available for this position. How to Apply Interested applicants should complete the J-PAL/IPA Common Application and: Select the Policy job category Choose Senior Policy Manager – J-PAL Europe – Zurich Upload: Updated CV Cover Letter Include details regarding: Relevant professional experience Qualifications Authorization to work in Switzerland Applications should be submitted no later than August 16, 2026 . Why You Should Apply This is an exceptional opportunity to contribute to one of the world s leading organizations dedicated to evidence-based policymaking. Working alongside Nobel Prize-winning economists Abhijit Banerjee and Esther Duflo, the successful candidate will help shape policies that improve lives around the world. Professionals interested in public policy, international development, economics, strategic partnerships, and research communication will find this role both intellectually rewarding and globally impactful. Application Portal Application Portal: https://bit.ly/4vEUXGX Application Dead line: August 16, 2026 Conclusion The Senior Policy Manager position at J-PAL Europe offers experienced policy professionals the chance to work at the highest levels of international development and evidence-informed policymaking. Based in Zurich, the role combines strategic leadership, partnership management, fundraising, policy communication, and collaboration with world-renowned researchers to influence public policy across Europe and beyond. If you have the required experience, outstanding communication skills, and authorization to work in Switzerland, this is a unique opportunity to make a lasting global impact. title: Global Youth Water Envoys Program 2026: Fully Funded Opportunity for 100 Young Leaders to Participate in the UN Water Conference in Abu Dhabi
summary: Applications are now open for the Global Youth Water Envoys Program 2026 , an international leadership and capacity-building initiative that will prepare 100 outstanding young leaders from around the world to actively contribute to global water governance and participate in the 2026 United Nations Water Conference in Abu Dhabi. Established by the Arab Youth Center in strategic partnership with the United Arab Emirates Ministry of Foreign Affairs , the program is designed to ensure that youth are not merely observers of global decision-making processes but meaningful contributors to international water policy discussions and solutions. The initiative is being launched ahead of the 2026 UN Water Conference , which will be co-hosted by the United Arab Emirates and the Republic of Senegal in Abu Dhabi this December and has received endorsement from the Conference Presidencies. Rather than limiting young people to symbolic participation, the Global Youth Water Envoys Program places selected participants at the center of international dialogue, equipping them with the knowledge, skills, mentorship, and opportunities needed to engage directly with policymakers, national delegations, experts, and institutional partners working to address one of the world s most urgent challenges—water security. About the Global Youth Water Envoys Program The Global Youth Water Envoys Program is an intensive leadership development initiative that combines capacity building, diplomatic engagement, policy dialogue, and international collaboration. The program will select 100 young leaders representing different regions of the world and guide them through a structured learning journey that includes: Foundational training Advanced thematic specialization Leadership development Policy engagement Direct interaction with governments and international organizations Participation in the UN Water Conference The program aims to create a new generation of youth leaders capable of influencing water governance at national, regional, and international levels. Why the Program Matters Water insecurity continues to be one of the greatest development challenges facing the world. Millions of people—particularly across developing countries—continue to experience water scarcity, poor sanitation, climate-related water challenges, and unequal access to safe water resources. Communities throughout the Global South are disproportionately affected by these challenges, while young people often remain underrepresented in formal water governance and international policy-making processes. The Global Youth Water Envoys Program seeks to bridge this gap by creating a structured platform where youth expertise, innovation, lived experiences, and policy contributions directly influence global discussions and international cooperation. The initiative reflects the United Arab Emirates commitment to: Strengthening international partnerships. Promoting regional cooperation. Empowering youth leadership. Supporting sustainable development. Advancing innovative solutions for global water challenges. Program Structure Selected participants will progress through several phases designed to prepare them for meaningful engagement at the global level. These include: Comprehensive online learning. Capacity-building sessions. Expert-led workshops. Specialized thematic training. Interactive dialogue preparation. Diplomatic engagement. Networking with institutional partners. Participation in international policy discussions. Throughout the program, participants will work alongside experts and policymakers while strengthening their leadership, negotiation, communication, and collaboration skills. Responsibilities of Global Youth Water Envoys Participants selected for the program will be expected to actively engage throughout the entire program. Their responsibilities include: Completing all required training sessions. Participating in engagement activities. Dedicating approximately 3–4 hours each week throughout the active program period. Contributing to their assigned Interactive Dialogue. Demonstrating professionalism, collaboration, and respect. Representing youth voices during discussions on global water governance. Participating in major program milestones. Taking part in the 2026 UN Water Conference , subject to logistical arrangements. Engaging with policymakers, experts, institutional leaders, and international organizations. Supporting discussions across the Conference s six Interactive Dialogues . Promoting knowledge exchange and long-term policy development. What Participants Will Gain The program offers exceptional professional and leadership development opportunities. Successful applicants will: Join an international network of 100 Global Youth Water Envoys . Receive world-class training from leading water sector experts. Benefit from mentorship throughout the program. Contribute to developing the Global Youth Water Statement . Build professional relationships with policymakers and international organizations. Expand their international professional network. Participate in the 2026 UN Water Conference in Abu Dhabi. Become members of an international alumni community. Access future collaboration and engagement opportunities after completing the program. Who Should Apply? The program welcomes applications from passionate young people interested in solving global water challenges and contributing to international policy discussions. Applicants should: Be between 18 and 35 years old . Demonstrate proficiency in English, the official working language of the program. Have an interest in or experience related to: Water governance Public policy Climate action Sustainability Innovation Finance Environmental development Other water-related disciplines Be committed to completing the entire program. Be willing to represent youth voices within their communities and internationally. Selection is based on merit, with priority given to applicants from water-stressed regions identified by the Water Stress Country Index, particularly: Africa Arab States Asia and the Pacific Nevertheless, applications from young leaders around the world are encouraged. Selection Process The Global Youth Water Envoys Program is committed to ensuring broad geographic representation while maintaining a competitive merit-based selection process. The organizers seek participants who demonstrate: Leadership potential. Passion for sustainable development. Commitment to water security. Interest in international cooperation. Ability to contribute meaningfully to policy discussions. Application Deadline The deadline to submit applications is: 13 August 2026 (12:00 AM Abu Dhabi Time). Interested applicants are encouraged to complete their applications well before the deadline to avoid last-minute technical issues. Apply HERE Applicants can learn more about the Global Youth Water Envoys Program HERE Conclusion The Global Youth Water Envoys Program 2026 presents a remarkable opportunity for young leaders worldwide to become active contributors to international water governance and sustainable development. Through expert training, mentorship, diplomatic engagement, and direct participation in the 2026 UN Water Conference, participants will gain the knowledge, networks, and experience needed to shape global conversations on one of humanity s most pressing challenges. If you are passionate about water security, climate action, public policy, or sustainable development, this program offers an outstanding platform to amplify your voice and make a lasting impact. Be sure to submit your application before the deadline of 13 August 2026 to be considered for this prestigious global initiative.
